name: Wartortle
route: Old docks, marsh channels, drowned basements, reed beds, irrigation cuts, submerged roads, and protected Squirtle nesting ruins
habitat: Connected freshwater territory with patrol channels, air pockets, submerged boundary markers, and young colony members nearby
behaviorTemperament: Deliberate, territorial, observant, and protective. Wartortle may save a human from drowning but may also refuse to release them until it decides the threat has passed.
diet: Aquatic plants, fish, shellfish, insects, small amphibians, carrion, stolen food, and prey driven into water channels
activityPattern: Crepuscular and nocturnal patrol activity, with daylight basking in hidden reeds or submerged rooms
bestTimeToFind: Dawn near old flood ruins, quiet docks, and reed lanes where pale ears or a tail drift at the surface
observationDifficulty: High. Wartortle usually notices observers first and can control the encounter from underwater.
populationStatus: Low-density but stable in mature Squirtle colonies and protected floodplain preserves
description: A bipedal blue turtle Pokemon with a reinforced shell, large tail, ear plumes, and expanded water-storage anatomy. In realistic field terms, it is a territorial water sentinel.
lore:
Wartortle is what the river keeps after it stops playing.

A Squirtle may splash at a child from the reeds. A Wartortle watches the childâ€™s parents first.

That is the difference.

The young stage is curious, social, and dangerously innocent. Wartortle is not innocent. It remembers. Not in the human way, perhaps, but in the old animal way that remembers which banks collapse, which boats carry nets, which voices bring food, and which footsteps mean hooks. A wild Wartortle can recognize a shoreline it has not visited in years. It can return to the same submerged doorway after floods rearrange the whole riverbed. It can find the bone pile under silt.

Old fishermen called them blue widowers because they appeared after drownings, sitting half-submerged in the reeds while searchers dragged the water. Their pale tails and ears would float like weed fronds, barely moving. Sometimes they watched for days. Sometimes, after the family gave up, the body would surface.

Sometimes it would not.

Modern rangers believe Wartortle often recover the drowned and move them away from active nesting sites. Villagers believed something darker: that Wartortle decided which dead were allowed to return.

A Wartortleâ€™s shell is more than armor. It is a pressure vessel, a memory vault, and a warning drum. When threatened, Wartortle can pull water into internal chambers and release it in sudden bursts powerful enough to break ribs, shatter wooden oars, or knock a grown person backward into deep current. But before it attacks, it usually drums.

Three hollow knocks from beneath a dock.

A slow tapping against a boat hull.

A sound like stones rolling inside an empty well.

If you hear that at night, you are already too close.

Unlike Squirtle, Wartortle do not always retreat from humans. They assess. They circle. They position themselves between intruders and water exits, forcing panic in the direction they want. A lone Wartortle can herd a person across mud flats with short jets of water at the feet. A pair can flip a canoe without surfacing. A colony guardian can keep an entire search party away from a nest simply by striking the underside of the dock until the old boards begin to fail.

They rarely waste effort.

This is what frightens experienced handlers most. Wartortle does not rage like some predators. It does not shriek, posture, or bluff for long. Its violence is deliberate. It has learned that water does not need anger to kill.

The long ears and tail are often mistaken for decoration, especially by children. In truth, they are sensory organs and stabilizers. A resting Wartortle may let the plumes drift on the surface, reading minute changes in current. Footsteps on a dock, a fish struggling in a net, a human slipping on mud, a child dragging a stick through shallows: all of it reaches the plumes before it reaches the eyes.

This gives rise to one of the oldest shoreline sayings:

If you can see a Wartortleâ€™s eyes, it is watching you.

If you can only see its tail, it already knows where you are.

Wartortle colonies form around old Squirtle nests, but they do not behave like simple family groups. The young splash, steal, chirp, and explore. Wartortle patrol the borders. They move through culverts, drowned basements, reed channels, flooded mine shafts, and irrigation ditches with the patience of things that know every route by touch.

In abandoned settlements, they become especially strange.

Divers have found Wartortle nesting in places where people once stored records, money, weapons, or keepsakes. Their dens often include objects arranged by weight and shape: locks, plates, tools, dolls, prayer beads, belt buckles, broken eyeglasses, shell casings, and polished stones. No one knows whether they understand these objects or simply use them for structure.

One ranger found an old Wartortle den in the flooded records office of Old Harrow Quay. The nest was built from ledger books, roof tiles, and the brass nameplates of the dead.

At the center was a childâ€™s school slate, scrubbed clean by water.

Wartortle are also known for rescue behavior, but it is more unsettling than Squirtleâ€™s. A Squirtle may drag a struggling human toward water because it mistakes submersion for safety. A Wartortle understands that humans breathe air. It will lift a drowning person toward the surface if it chooses to. It will push them toward shore. It will hold their head above water with surprising gentleness.

But it may also hold them there and not let them leave.

Several survivors of river accidents describe waking on mud banks with a Wartortle sitting nearby, one heavy forelimb resting across their chest like a restraint. When they tried to rise, the Wartortle pressed harder. When rescuers approached, it hissed and sprayed water at their feet. Some were held until dawn. Some until rain stopped. Some until another Wartortle arrived and inspected them.

One survivor wrote: â€œIt saved me like I was property.â€

This is the heart of Wartortle lore. It protects what it claims. That protection may save a life, preserve a nest, defend a ruin, or imprison a trespasser until the river decides what they are worth.

Old Kanto communities treated Wartortle with cautious respect. They left fish at certain stones. They rang bells before crossing flooded roads. They taught children never to whistle at pale tails in the water. In some villages, if a person drowned and the body was not recovered within three days, the family would place a bowl of clean water on the doorstep and whisper the personâ€™s name into it. The belief was that a Wartortle might carry the name downstream and release the dead.

Field researchers dismiss this as mourning ritual.

Rangers are less quick to laugh.

There are too many cases where bodies surfaced after the bowl was placed.

Too many where the water outside the house was found disturbed by small, webbed tracks.

Too many where something blue watched from the ditch until sunrise.

The official field rules for Wartortle territory are harsher than for Squirtle:

Do not enter old flood ruins alone.

Do not step over lines of shells, stones, or stolen metal objects.

Do not follow tapping under docks.

Do not assume a rescued person is free to leave until the Wartortle leaves first.

And never strike a Squirtle in sight of a Wartortle.

The young may forgive.

The guardian stage remembers.

loreSummary: A guardian stage tied to drowned ruins, recovered bodies, boundary rituals, and the idea that a trainer becomes territory that walks.
survivalUse: Can defend camps near water, tow injured swimmers, locate underwater routes, break light debris with pressure bursts, and warn of flood-channel movement.
handlingWarning: Do not cross object lines, touch young Squirtle, enter old flood ruins alone, or try to pull away from a guarding Wartortle by force.
encounterAdvice: Stop, lower your hands, back away from the waterline, and leave by the same path. If it blocks you, choose another path rather than challenging it.
firstAid: Assess rib trauma, ankle injuries, hypothermia, near-drowning, and blunt-force impact from pressure bursts or shell strikes.
companionSuitability: Good for experienced handlers who can accept territorial behavior
fieldBehaviors: Positions itself between intruders and exits
Uses tail and ears to read currents
Moves bodies or objects away from nests
Guards injured humans like claimed property
Removes survey flags or unfamiliar markers
signsOfPresence: Pale ear plumes drifting like weeds
Large webbed tracks beside smaller Squirtle tracks
Fresh shell scrapes on submerged doors
Quiet water where birds avoid landing
fieldNote: If you can only see the tail or ear plumes, assume it already knows your location and is measuring your movement through the water.
originStory:
Wartortle was formally documented in 1824, twelve years after the Squirtle colony at Old Harrow Quay was accepted into the scientific record.

For years after the 1812 survey, researchers believed the larger blue figures seen near Squirtle nesting grounds were simply old Squirtle. This mistake nearly killed the second Harrow expedition.

The original Squirtle colony had been monitored from a distance after the chapel-bell discovery. Young specimens were seen in flooded houses, reed channels, and the collapsed market district. But local fishermen continued reporting something else: larger shapes that moved only at dawn, with pale ears like torn flags and tails that trailed behind them in the current.

The fishermen insisted these were not Squirtle.

The naturalists insisted fishermen exaggerated.

In winter of 1823, three researchers entered the drowned district to collect eggshell fragments from the chapel loft. They waited until the Squirtle colony left to feed. By their own account, the site was quiet. Too quiet. No splashing. No chirping. No young visible in the rafters.

The first warning was a hollow knock from beneath their boat.

The second was another knock from the opposite side.

The third came from inside the chapel.

A Wartortle surfaced between the pews.

The expedition notes describe it as â€œa mature shelled water beast of Squirtle character, but with a longer tail, enlarged auditory plumes, heavier shell, and a stare more deliberate than any juvenile specimen.â€ It did not attack immediately. It watched them remove the eggshell fragments. Then it struck the boat from below hard enough to split a plank.

One researcher drowned in the chapel nave.

A second survived by clinging to the bell rope.

The third was found at dawn on the roof of the old post office, alive but hypothermic, with bruises around both ankles. He claimed the Wartortle had dragged him there after the boat sank. He also claimed it sat beside him all night and struck him with water every time he tried to climb down.

His account was dismissed as shock.

Then the shell marks were found in the mud below.

In 1824, the Crown Natural Society returned with better equipment, shore guards, and one of the original Squirtle handlers from the 1812 survey. This time, they did not enter the chapel first. They waited at the boundary and placed fish on a half-submerged stone used by locals as an offering site.

At sunset, a Wartortle surfaced.

The handler recognized it by a notch in its shell. It had been one of the juvenile Squirtle observed twelve years earlier.

This proved that the larger shoreline guardian was not a separate species and not merely an aged variant. It was the next evolutionary stage.

The discovery established the second point in the Squirtle-line chronology:

1812: Squirtle formally recorded at Old Harrow Quay.

1824: Wartortle confirmed after a marked juvenile from the original colony was identified as an evolved guardian.

The early Wartortle studies revealed several important changes from Squirtle. The shell thickened and became more structurally complex. The limbs strengthened for both swimming and short land movement. The tail and ears developed into current-sensitive plumes. The water-storage system expanded, allowing stronger bursts and longer patrols away from open water.

Behavior changed even more dramatically.

Squirtle behaved like colony children.

Wartortle behaved like border guards.

They patrolled outer channels, repaired nest structures, recovered stolen eggs, and drove off predators. They also showed selective tolerance toward humans. Fishermen who left offerings were ignored. Egg collectors were attacked. Children were watched but not always harmed. Armed surveyors were met with immediate threat displays.

One early naturalist wrote that Wartortle possessed â€œthe moral character of a locked gate.â€

This was meant as mockery, but later ranger manuals adopted the phrase.

The species seemed to understand boundaries. Not laws, not property, not human rights, but boundaries in the old territorial sense. Nest. Bank. Ruin. Current. Young. Dead. Stranger. Threat. Claimed.

Everything belonged to one of those categories.

The hardest part of studying Wartortle was that the PokÃ©mon changed the study site faster than humans could map it. Flooded rooms were blocked with debris. New channels appeared overnight. Marked stones vanished. Survey flags were removed and stacked neatly on the offering rock. Once, a researcherâ€™s notebook disappeared from a dock and reappeared three days later inside a Squirtle nest, packed in mud with the pages pressed flat.

No one admitted being impressed.

Everyone was impressed.

The origin theory that developed after 1824 expanded Mara Vellâ€™s earlier Squirtle hypothesis. If Squirtle evolved to exploit flooded human spaces as nurseries, Wartortle evolved to defend those nurseries from everything that came after: predators, scavengers, egg thieves, surveyors, and returning humans who wanted their ruins back.

This made Wartortle one of the first PokÃ©mon formally described as a post-disaster territorial specialist.

It did not simply survive in ruined habitats.

It organized them.

The long-standing folklore of â€œwater sentriesâ€ began to make sense. Villagers had not imagined guardian spirits after floods. They had seen Wartortle patrolling the borders of new aquatic territory. The bells, offerings, carved shells, and doorstep water bowls were not random superstition. They were human attempts to communicate with an animal that already understood ritual through repetition.

Feed this stone, pass safely.

Cross that marker, be warned.

Take from the nest, be taken.

The most controversial record from this period concerns the drowned chapel bell. After the 1824 expedition, researchers tried to remove the bell for preservation. The local villagers objected, saying the bell no longer belonged to them. The Society ignored them.

That night, every boat tied near the old quay was cut loose.

The next morning, the bell had fallen through the rotten chapel floor into the flooded nave below. Its bronze surface was later found wedged beneath collapsed stone, surrounded by shells, bones, and stolen metal objects arranged in a ring.

The Society marked the bell as unrecoverable.

The villagers marked the place as settled.

Wartortle remains the stage where the Squirtle line stops being charming and starts being sovereign. It is still capable of loyalty. It can still bond with trainers. It may defend a human with terrifying dedication. But the bond is never simple ownership. To a Wartortle, a trainer is not master.

A trainer is territory that walks.

And territory must be guarded, corrected, retrieved, and sometimes dragged back from places it should not have gone.

Squirtle is the child at the riverâ€™s edge.

Wartortle is the hand around your ankle.

Blastoise is what waits where the river meets the sea.
originSummary: Confirmed in 1824 after a marked juvenile from the 1812 Old Harrow Quay Squirtle colony was identified as an evolved guardian.

evolutionLine.name: Squirtle
evolutionLine.note: Tiny Turtle Water-type. Juvenile shoreline colony member with shell withdrawal and pressurized mouth-spray behavior.

evolutionLine.name: Wartortle
evolutionLine.note: Turtle Water-type. Territorial guardian stage with stronger limbs, longer tail, ear plumes, and heavier water-pressure biology.

evolutionLine.name: Blastoise
evolutionLine.note: Shellfish Water-type. Final armored pressure-cannon stage with Mega and Gigantamax battle phenomena.

mainLine.name: Squirtle
mainLine.note: Juvenile shoreline survivor. Shell hardens after hatching and water projection begins early.

mainLine.name: Wartortle
mainLine.note: Guardian stage. Ear and tail plumes help balance and current-sensing during fast swimming.

mainLine.name: Blastoise
mainLine.note: Final heavy-shell stage. Carries high-pressure shell cannons and functions as a living harbor weapon.

target.name: Mega Blastoise
target.note: Temporary form with intensified cannon output, Mega Launcher ability, and increased shell mass.

target.name: Gigantamax Blastoise
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat water output and recoil forces as disaster-scale hazards.

domesticationStatus: Trainable but territorial, best handled as a partner-guardian rather than an obedient pet
prey: Fish, amphibians, aquatic insects, small mammals near water, carrion, and food taken from shoreline camps
territoryRange: Usually 2 to 10 square miles of connected water, bank, culvert, ruin, and nursery channels
lifeCycle: Evolves from Squirtle at level 16, expands water-storage organs and sensory plumes, and evolves into Blastoise at level 36.
containmentRequirements: Large aquatic enclosure with patrol routes, visual boundaries, dry basking ledges, no public shoreline access, and enrichment objects that can be arranged without harming plumbing.
ecologicalRole: Defends Squirtle colonies, controls aquatic scavengers, organizes flood debris, and stabilizes nesting boundaries in post-disaster wetlands.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Tail-fur fungus, algae imbalance, shell infections, waterborne parasites, and joint strain from poor land surfaces are concerns.
reproductionNesting: Does not serve as the primary breeding stage in field colonies, but defends egg routes, nursery chambers, and juvenile feeding lanes.
predators: Large Water-types, coordinated humans, territorial Blastoise, and rare apex river predators
emergencyResponse: Do not enter the water to negotiate. Remove bystanders, retreat from the marked line, wait for Water-type handlers, and avoid lifting claimed objects from the nest zone.
legalStatus: High-risk Water-type permit recommended or required, especially near public water infrastructure
lifespan: Wild: several years before evolution or territorial displacement. Managed care: long-lived with clean water, shell care, and low-stress boundaries.
socialStructure: Guardian of Squirtle colonies. Less playful than the juvenile stage, but capable of intense pair-bonding with trusted handlers.

fieldCapabilities.waterPressureLevel: Strong enough to injure at close range and break weak wood
fieldCapabilities.effectiveSprayRange: About 20 to 40 ft for aimed pressure bursts
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Strong aquatic sprint with moderate land movement
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Excellent in water, limited on dry land
fieldCapabilities.balance: Highly stable in water due to tail and ear plume control

trainingRisk: Restraint injuries, near-drowning, overprotective guarding, pressure-burst trauma, tail-fur disease, and territorial refusal to release people or objects
physiology: Wartortle has a reinforced shell, expanded water-storage chambers, stronger limbs, and sensory ear and tail plumes that detect current changes. Its pressure bursts are more deliberate than Squirtle spray.

keyFacts.tailLength: Long furred tail used for balance, signaling, and current sensing
keyFacts.habitat: Flood ruins, marsh channels, docks, and nursery waterways
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild and managed lifespans vary, but folklore links this stage with extreme longevity.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually one or a few guardians per Squirtle nursery zone

domesticationDifficulty: High. Bonds deeply but treats people, gear, and paths as controllable territory.
physicalCharacteristics: Indigo-blue bipedal turtle body, cream belly, heavier shell, clawed limbs, pale ear plumes, and a large furred tail that deepens with age.
dietRestrictions: Avoid polluted standing water, excessive salt, low-protein diets during growth, and confined tanks that damage the tail or shell edges.

realPokemonData.evolutionRequirement: Evolves from Squirtle at level 16 and into Blastoise at level 36

realismNotes.interpretation: Game powers are treated as biological pressure, shell, swimming, and weather-response systems that could plausibly exist in a field-guide version of the real world.
realismNotes.keptGameRequirements: Water type, Torrent, Rain Dish as hidden ability, Squirtle to Wartortle at level 16, Wartortle to Blastoise at level 36, and Blastoise special forms.
realismNotes.safetyFraming: Water projection and shell impact are treated as physical hazards, with drowning and structural damage handled as the primary real-world risks.

researchSources.name: Pokemon.com PokÃ©dex
researchSources.usedFor: Official species category, height, weight, type, and listed standard ability.

researchSources.name: PokÃ©mon Database
researchSources.usedFor: National numbers, hidden ability, base stats, type defenses, move references, and evolution levels.

researchSources.name: User provided lore
researchSources.usedFor: Dark lore, origin stories, historical discovery chronology, and real-life field-guide tone.

researchSources.name: User provided sample JSON
researchSources.usedFor: Required structure, field names, and Field PokÃ©dex style.
